papers.title: The Runaway White Dwarf LP400-22 Has a Companion
papers.author_names: Mukremin Kilic Warren R. Brown Carlos Allende Prieto B. Swift S. J. Kenyon J. Liebert M. A. Agueros

abstract.text: We report the detection of a radial velocity companion to the extremely low mass white dwarf LP400-22. The radial velocity of the white dwarf shows variations with a semi-amplitude of 119 km/s and a 0.98776 day period, which implies a companion mass of M > 0.37 Msun. The optical photometry rules out a main sequence companion. Thus the invisible companion is another white dwarf or a neutron star. Using proper motion measurements and the radial velocity of the binary system, we find that it has an unusual Galactic orbit. LP400-22 is moving away from the Galactic center with a velocity of 396 km/s, which is very difficult to explain by supernova runaway ejection mechanisms. Dynamical interactions with a massive black hole like that in the Galactic center can in principle explain its peculiar velocity, if the progenitor was a triple star system comprised of a close binary and a distant tertiary companion. Until better proper motions become available, we consider LP400-22 to be most likely a halo star with a very unusual orbit.

source_fields.sections_text: 5. DISCUSSION: LP400–22 is a binary system with a 0.98776-day orbital period and a high space velocity of 398 ± 50 km s⁻¹, indicating it is a halo star with kinematics distinct from typical halo or disk runaways, ruling out supernova kicks and SNe Ia origins, and suggesting it is not a disk runaway or Galactic center ejection. 6. CONCLUSIONS: LP400–22 likely has a white dwarf companion and is part of a rare group of halo white dwarfs with unusual orbits, supporting a binary formation scenario for extremely low-mass white dwarfs. 1. INTRODUCTION: This paper presents the first radial velocity detection of a companion to the ultra-low-mass white dwarf LP400–22, providing critical observational evidence to test binary mass-loss scenarios and constrain the formation pathways of extremely low-mass white dwarfs. 2. OBSERVATIONS: This section describes the high-precision radial velocity measurements of LP400−22 obtained through moderate-resolution spectroscopy, with velocities derived from self-cross-correlation of co-added spectra and validated through multiple independent methods, yielding reliable results with systematic errors below 10 km s⁻¹. 4. THE NATURE OF THE COMPANION: The companion to LP400–22 is likely a low-mass white dwarf formed through a binary evolution involving a common envelope phase, with no detectable optical signature ruling out a main-sequence companion, and the system's properties are consistent with a 0.48 M⊙ white dwarf companion originating from a 2.0 M⊙ progenitor. 3. LP400$-$22 AND ITS COMPANION: The radial velocity variations of LP400$-$22 reveal a binary companion with a circular orbit of period 0.98776 days and radial velocity amplitude 118.7 km s⁻¹, while spectroscopic analysis confirms it as an extremely low-mass white dwarf with effective temperature of 11290 ± 50 K and surface gravity log g = 6.30 ± 0.02.

abstract: LP400-22 is moving away from the Galactic center with a velocity of 396 km/s, which is very difficult to explain by supernova runaway ejection mechanisms.
title: The Runaway White Dwarf LP400-22 Has a Companion

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper reports detection of radial velocity companion to specific runaway white dwarf LP400-22, presents observational data including radial velocity variations, proper motion measurements, and orbital parameters.
